my_thoughts: The rejection/validation works: not necessarily lazy, philosophical = sometimes right (depends on object), practical = sometimes only way (for valuation). The student asked moral judgment question (seeking validation for laziness), which shows they understand: philosophical thinking = lazy (moral judgment). They'll likely ask about categories next.
accumulated_insight: T2: Student asked moral judgment question ('Is it lazy to use philosophical thinking?')—seeking validation for laziness via asks for moral judgment and seeks categorical status. They understand: philosophical thinking = lazy (moral judgment). The comparison_contrast mechanism rejects: not necessarily lazy, philosophical = sometimes right (depends on object), practical = sometimes only way (for valuation) ('Not necessarily. Sometimes there are things you can take only philosophically, others only philosophically and theoretically. There are things to which we have no practical approach and for which we must find analogies. So sometimes it is quite right. But there are things which you can take only practically, for only then can you value them'). Pattern: authority question → internal authority → laziness question → rejection/validation → [next: categories question]. They're grasping: philosophical = sometimes right, practical = sometimes only way (rejection/validation).
visible_response:
Not necessarily.
Sometimes there are things you can take only philosophically,
others only philosophically and theoretically.
There are things to which we have no
practical approach and for which we must find analogies.
So sometimes it is quite
right. But there are things which you can take only practically, for only then can you
value them.
